coredata.dc:title: Facile, green and energy-efficient preparation of fluorescent carbon dots from processed traditional Chinese medicine and their applications for on-site semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr(VI)

coredata.dc:description: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from biological sources have attracted much attention because of their low cost, excellent bio-compatibility and renewability. However, previously reported synthesis strategies of biomass-derived CDs were commonly involved in the sophisticated equipment, high energy consumption and complicated synthetic conditions, restricting their practical application in various fields. In this work, a green, facile and low energy consumption method was demonstrated to prepare highly fluorescent CDs through room temperature one-step solvent extraction from rice fried Codonopsis pilosula (CP) which could be easily obtained commercially. The obtained CP-CDs exhibited outstanding fluorescence property (quantum yield up to 12.8 %) and high photostability. Besides, CP-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence response to Cr(VI) over a broad range of 0.03−50 μM and a satisfactory detection limit of 15 nM. The developed sensing system was applied to quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in environment water samples and industrial sewage successfully with good recovery and high precision. More interestingly, CP-CDs based fluorescent hydrogel sensor was further fabricated for on-site semi-quantitative visual Cr(VI) detection in industrial sewage, demonstrating their tremendous potential for the fast and low-cost monitoring of Cr(VI) in industrial discharges.

head.ce:title: Facile, green and energy-efficient preparation of fluorescent carbon dots from processed traditional Chinese medicine and their applications for on-site semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr(VI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from biological sources have attracted much attention because of their low cost, excellent bio-compatibility and renewability. However, previously reported synthesis strategies of biomass-derived CDs were commonly involved in the sophisticated equipment, high energy consumption and complicated synthetic conditions, restricting their practical application in various fields. In this work, a green, facile and low energy consumption method was demonstrated to prepare highly fluorescent CDs through room temperature one-step solvent extraction from rice fried Codonopsis pilosula (CP) which could be easily obtained commercially. The obtained CP-CDs exhibited outstanding fluorescence property (quantum yield up to 12.8 %) and high photostability. Besides, CP-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence response to Cr(VI) over a broad range of 0.03−50 μM and a satisfactory detection limit of 15 nM. The developed sensing system was applied to quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in environment water samples and industrial sewage successfully with good recovery and high precision. More interestingly, CP-CDs based fluorescent hydrogel sensor was further fabricated for on-site semi-quantitative visual Cr(VI) detection in industrial sewage, demonstrating their tremendous potential for the fast and low-cost monitoring of Cr(VI) in industrial discharges.

ce:section.ce:para: For the past decades, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have become the promising photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterials in the fields of bioimaging [1–3], sensing [4–7], drug delivery [8–10] and photovoltaic devices [11,12], because of their exciting attributes such as excellent photostability, good hydrophilicity and high PL quantum yield. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots comprising toxic metallic elements such as Cd2+ and Pb2+, CDs are less toxic and highly biocompatible, which are thus considered much safer for biomedical applications. The top-down and bottom-up methods are widely used to prepare CDs [5,13,14]. Compared with the former, the bottom-up methods are more popular choices for their advantages such as high yield, low cost, mild reaction conditions and easier purification processes of the product [15–17].

The commonly used bottom-up approaches for the synthesis of CDs include thermal decomposition [18], hydrothermal treatment [19], plasma [20], microwave [21] and ultrasonic treatments [22]. Among these strategies, hydrothermal and pyrolytic synthesis are the most attractive methods owing to their relatively simple and efficient preparation processes. Especially, the previous reports about hydrothermal or pyrolytic synthesis approaches of green CDs from the plant- or animal-derived bio-resources (e.g., peach gum polysaccharide [23], peanut shells [24], cow milk [25] and olive pit [26]) have attracted much attention and utilized for different applications recently because of the regenerative resources and chemical-free procedure [27]. However, the high energy consumption is unavoidable due to the high temperature reaction conditions and time-consuming heating processes [28–30]. Meanwhile, the product yield and quantum yield of obtained green CDs are not satisfactory in most cases. As a result, the application feasibility of green CDs is reduced because of the un-green and high energy-consuming synthetic approaches. Therefore, it’s necessary to develop alternative green synthetic approaches of CDs based on green precursors.

Traditional Chinese medicines (TCM) are a type of biomass material produced by processing plants, animals or another biomass. Using TCM as the carbon sources to synthesize CDs is an important strategy for the preparation of functional CDs [31,32]. Chang’s group has prepared fluorescent CDs from ginger for cell imaging by hydrothermal synthesis at 300 ℃ [32]. Similarly, Trapa bispinosa peel was also utilized to synthesize luminescent CDs with favorable water solubility and good biocompatibility [33]. Biomass-derived CDs was synthesized by Chen and co-workers using purple perilla as the carbon source and served as the probe for the detection of silver ion successfully [34]. However, the relevant researches were commonly performed via pyrolysis or hydrothermal method to prepare CDs. The defects such as expensive heating devices and high energy consumption still existed. On the other hand, it is interesting to note that many TCM preparation processes involve the use of heating processing method to realize some degree of carbonization of TCM, which are very similar with the hydrothermal and pyrolytic synthesis process of CDs. Thus, it would be reasonable to speculate that some processed products of TCM encompass large amounts of fluorescent CDs.

Inspired by this, we firstly proposed a green, facile and low energy consumption strategy using the one-step solvent extraction treatment to prepare green CDs from processed TCM under room temperature. For a proof-of-concept demonstration, the rice fried Codonopsis pilosula (CP), which was one of the commonly used processed TCM and could be easily obtained commercially, was utilized as the model processed TCM derived bio-resource to fabricate highly fluorescent CDs. Compared with other synthesis strategies of green CDs which was involved in hydrothermal or pyrolysis treatment and utilized biomass materials as the precursors, this method possessed remarkable advantages such as more convenient operation, lower energy consumption and more economical synthesis costs because it needed no expensive heating devices and time-consuming high temperature heating processes. The obtained rice fried CP-derived CDs (CP-CDs) exhibited excellent fluorescence property (quantum yield up to 12.8 %) and high photostability without any passivation or functionalization in CDs surface. Furthermore, the CP-CDs exhibited a high selectivity toward Cr(VI) with sensitive fluorescence response across a broad range of 0.03−50 μM and a low detection limit of 15 nM, which are practical for the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in the environment water samples and industrial sewage with good recovery and high precision. The possible quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) to CP-CDs was also investigated systematically. Based on their excellent fluorescence and sensing characteristics, CP-CDs were further utilized to fabricate fluorescent hydrogel sensor for the on-site semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr(VI) in industrial waste water with a portable UV lamp, which effectively improved their practicability for sensing application. This research not only develops a new green and energy-saving methodology for fabricating fluorescent CDs and expanding the application potential of processed TCM resources, but also holds great promise for application in the fields of sensing, environmental monitoring and biomedical applications.

The rice fried Codonopsis pilosula was purchased from Tianzhi traditional Chinese medicine Co., Ltd (Luzhou, Sichuan, China), which accorded with the quality standards of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. It should be emphasized that as a commonly used adjuvant in TCM processing method, the residual rice was completely isolated from the fried Codonopsis pilosula before sale. Potassium dichromate, ferric chloride, copper sulfate, silver nitrate and other metal ions were all bought from Huaxia Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium acetate, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid and methanol were of analytical grade and all obtained from Sichuan Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate and agarose were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis membranes were supplied by Yuanye Biotech Co., Ltd. The AWL-1002-H ultrapure water system was used to prepare ultrapure water.

The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were characterized by an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600) manufactured by Shimadzu Corporation. A LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) was utilized to obtain fluorescence properties of the CP-CDs. The type of functional groups on the surface of CP-CDs were identified by FTIR spectra using IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The elemental component analysis of CP-CDs was performed by an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Electron, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed by an electron microscope (JEM-1200EX, accelerating voltage,120 kV). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained by a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was performed using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was done using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 system. The determination of Cr(VI) concentration in Cr(VI)-spiked environmental water samples and Cr(VI)-containing industrial wastewater was conducted using an Agilent 730 ICP-OES system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification processes of CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CP-CDs was conducted using the one-step ultrasonic-assisted solvent extraction method. Firstly, the rice fried Codonopsis pilosula was rinsed thoroughly with the ultrapure water to eliminate the possible rice-derived by-product formed during the processing procedure and dried in oven under 50 ℃. Then the dried rice fried Codonopsis pilosula (5 g) was smashed by a pulverizer, and the as-formed black powder of Codonopsis pilosula was uniformly dispersed to methanol (500 mL) in round-bottom flask (1000 mL). After 4 h of ultrasonic-assisted extraction at room temperature, a dark brown solution will be obtained and the insoluble precipitate was removed through centrifugation. The CDs contained in supernatant was treated by rotary evaporators to remove the residual methanol. The resulting concentrated solution was re-dispersed in ultrapure water and the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h via a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000 Da). Then the dialyzed solution was filtered through a membrane filter (0.22 μm) and lyophilized. The obtained powder of CP-CDs was preserved in refrigerator (4 ℃) for the further characterization and application. The whole preparation processes did not require any other passivating agents and purification operations.

The Cr(VI) detection was conducted in sodium acetate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0) solution. Briefly, a quartz cuvette was added with 10 μL of CP-CDs solution and then mixed with 100 μL of different concentration of Cr(VI) standard stock solutions. Subsequently, the sodium acetate buffer was added into the solutions and 4 mL diluent was obtained. After uniform mixing and incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence intensity of blank sample and different concentration of Cr(VI) additive samples were measured under 360 nm excitation, respectively. All the experiments were conducted at room temperature and repeated in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples and industrial sewage
ce:section.ce:para: The application of Cr(VI) detection in environmental water samples was carried out by using the lake, river and tap water samples, which were collected from Yanyu Lake (Luzhou, Sichuan, China), Tuojiang River and our laboratory, respectively. Prior to use, the residual chlorine was removed by boiled tap water sample for five minutes. The water samples from river and lake were centrifuged at the speed of 10 000 rpm for 10 min and then the insoluble components were removed by using 0.22 μm membrane filters. To calculate the recovery rates of Cr(VI) based on the presented detection method, the environmental water samples were added with different concentrations of Cr(VI) standard solutions. The industrial wastewater from vanadium extraction was first centrifuged and filtered to remove the insoluble components. Then, the industrial wastewater was diluted by 20 times using sodium acetate buffer. The concentration of Cr(VI) in the wastewater was further determined using the CP-CDs-based sensing system in aqueous solution according to the constructed standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CP-CDs doped hydrogel-based fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogel-based fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) determination are made from the mixture of CP-CDs and agarose via hydrogen bonding and dipole-dipole interactions. In a typical preparation process, agarose (0.2 g) was mixed with 10 mL CP-CDs solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0) and boiled for 5 min to make agarose dissolved completely. Then the obtained solution was sub-packed in round molds and cooled down to room temperature to get the CP-CDs hydrogel sheets with about 1.6 cm in diameter and 1.5 mm in height. Each CP-CDs sheet contained an average of 0.7 mg CP-CDs. The as-prepared CP-CDs hydrogel sheets would soak in the water samples for follow-on sensing tests.

Several common processed TCM, including charred Granati Pericarpium, fried Crataegi Fructus, charred Typhaee Pollen, fried Radix Paeoniae, stir-baked Fructus Gardeniae and rice fried CP, were used as the candidate bio-resources to prepare the possible fluorescent CDs. As listed in Table S1, all the six processed TCM could be utilized for the direct extraction of fluorescent CDs and the rice fried CP derived CDs exhibited the highest quantum yield among them (12.8 %, determined utilizing quinine sulfate as the reference). Therefore, the rice fried CP was selected as the model processed TCM derived bio-resource to fabricate highly fluorescent CDs. The whole synthetic procedure for the fabrication of CDs from rice fried Codonopsis pilosula was illustrated in Scheme 1 . CP-CDs with a relatively high quantum yield (12.8 %) and excellent photostability can be easily prepared via a facile and energy-efficient solvent extraction method at room temperature. We further summarized the synthesis conditions, equipment costs and quantum yields of some green CDs prepared via the traditional bottom-up methods and the presented CP-CDs [24,35–40]. As shown in Table S2, most of the green CDs are synthesized under high reaction temperature and long heating time, even more than 400 ℃, which means the expensive heating equipment, huge energy consumption and sophisticated operating steps are indispensable. Besides, their fluorescence quantum yields are also relatively low (<10 %), which are unfavorable for their practical applications. By contrast, The CP-CDs extracted directly from processed TCM under room temperature not only possess relatively high quantum yield, but also have other remarkable advantages such as more facile and environmentally friendly preparation process, more economical synthesis costs and lower energy consumption.

The morphology, surface groups and chemical components of CP-CDs were recorded using TEM and several other spectroscopy methods. First, the TEM and DLS measurements were performed for characterizing the morphology and size of CP-CDs in methanol and aqueous solution. As displayed in Fig. S1a and Fig. 1 a, the TEM images reveal the as-prepared CP-CDs were uniform, monodispersed spheres with narrow size distribution and the mean diameter about 9.60 and 11.54 nm in methanol and aqueous solution respectively (Fig. S1b and Fig. 1b). A well-arranged lattice fringes could be observed both in methanol and water dispersed CP-CDs with the interplanar distance of 0.24 nm under HR-TEM image (Fig. S1a and Fig. 1a inset), which proved that the physically extracted CP-CDs had similar graphite-like crystalline structure with those previously reported CDs obtained by chemical synthesis [7,13]. This finding was further verified by XRD patterns of methanol-dispersed CP-CDs. The results of XRD measurement shown in Fig. S1c exhibited a broad diffraction peak (2θ = 20°) and a weak diffraction peak (2θ = 38°), corresponding to (002) and (101) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon, respectively [41]. Besides, as shown in Fig. S2a, the hydrodynamic diameter of methanol-dispersed CP-CDs obtained by DLS measurement was mainly distributed from 18 to 50 nm and their average hydrodynamic diameter was around 27.2 nm, which were very close to the observed DLS results of water-dispersed CP-CDs illustrated in Fig. S2b (size distribution in the range from 21 to approximately 43 nm, average hydrodynamic diameter 28.8 nm). The DLS measurement was conducted in the wet state of CP-CDs and the TEM measurement was carried out in the collapse form of the CDs. Therefore, the size distribution observed from the DLS analysis is higher than that obtained from TEM, which is also consistent with the previously reported results [42]. Furthermore, the high degree of consistency in the size of water-dispersed and methanol-dispersed CP-CDs clearly demonstrated that CP-CDs was derived from the carbonization procedure of TCM processing rather than the possible aggregation of fluorescent substances after changing the solvent from methanol to water.

The surface functional groups of CP-CDs were identified by FTIR spectra measurements. The broad absorption band peaked at ∼3390 cm−1 can be assigned to the hydrophilic groups stretching vibration such as OH or NH [43], and the two characteristic absorbance bands centered at 2934 cm−1 and 2875 cm−1 indicate the presence of CH bonding of methylene on the CP-CDs (Fig. 1c). In addition, the two sharp peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1 can be ascribed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate groups [44], respectively, and the peak at 1340 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of OH [45]. Besides, the two peaks at 1057 cm−1 and 1251 cm−1 are corresponding to the COC, CC bending vibration [44,46], respectively. In summary, All the FTIR signals revealed that the obtained CDs had abundant oxygen, carbon and nitrogen moieties. The presences of various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CP-CDs such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups are conducive to enhance their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solution.

The XPS measurement was also utilized to investigated the functional groups and elements composition of CP-CDs. The full spectrum shown in Fig. 2 a exhibits two dominant peaks at 531.8 eV, 284.3 eV and a weak peak at 399.2 eV, which correspond to oxygen (25.22 %), carbon (72.08 %), and nitrogen (2.7 %), respectively. The large proportion of carbon element existed in CP-CDs is in line with the element analysis results of other CDs artificially synthesized via hydrothermal methods. The high-resolution C1 s peak (Fig. 2b) can be splitted into three peaks, confirming the presence of CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO/CN (286.2 eV), CO (287.8 eV) [47–49]. The high-resolution O1 s peak (Fig. 2c) can be fitted into three peaks at 530.8, 531.6, and 532.0 eV, corresponding to COH, CO, and COC, respectively [50,51]. Three major peaks in the N1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d) possess the binding energy at 398.3 eV for CN bond, 398.9 eV for pyridinic N bond and 400.4 eV for graphitic N [52]. The above XPS data are clearly coincided with the characterization results of FTIR.

Collectively, based on the characterization results of TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS, it can be confirmed that the obtained CP-CDs possess graphite-like crystalline structure decorated with abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl and amino groups. The possible formation mechanisms of CP-CDs are probably relevant to the Maillard reaction which are commonly occurred in the heating procedure of TCM processing. More specifically, in initial stages of the Maillard reaction, plenty of products such as ketosamine, N-substituted glycoside and reductones could be formed through the carbonyl ammonia condensation reaction between reducing sugars and ammonium compounds in Codonopsis pilosula. During the subsequent intermediate stages, a series of decarboxylation, deamination and molecular rearrangement reactions proceeded simultaneously to form various types of heterocyclic compounds such as pyridine, benzoylpyridine and pyrazine etc. Next, the obtained products would go through a sequence of condensation and polymerization reaction under the continuous heating processes, which resulted in the formation of aromatic clusters. The carbon nuclei emerged in the subsequent carbonization process of the formed clusters and grew rapidly when the amounts of aromatic clusters exceeded the critical supersaturation, resulting in the generation of graphite-like CP-CDs modified with abundant hydrophilic functional groups [44]. These hydrophilic functional groups endow CP-CDs with good water dispersibility, which is the cornerstone for their possible bio-medical or sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and photostability of CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CP-CDs were investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The black line in Fig. S3a exhibits the UV–vis absorption curve of CP-CDs in methanol. Two visible absorption peaks were clearly observed at 215 and 294 nm, respectively, which could be assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC and the n-π* transition of functional groups present on the CDs [53,54]. Similar absorption peaks located at about 225 and 290 nm were observed in CP-CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 3 a). The observed slight difference in the location of absorption peaks in different solvent are associated with the different transition energy levels resulted from solvent effect [55]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectrum of CP-CDs in methanol and ultrapure water were also investigated. After the solvent changed from methanol to water, the maximum emission of CP-CDs changed from 456 to 436 nm with 390 nm and 360 nm excitation, respectively. This phenomenon could be attributed to the surface state and band edge emission underwent opposite solvatochromic shifts when the solvent polarity was changed [56]. A strong blue fluorescence of CP-CDs could be observed both in methanol and water solution (Fig. 3a and Fig.S3a inset) when exposed to illumination of a UV lamp (365 nm). Besides, to further analyze the fluorescence behaviors of the CP-CDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of CP-CDs in methanol and aqueous solution were investigated under different excitation wavelengths with an increment of 10 nm. As depicted in Fig. 3b and Fig. S3b, the CP-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence properties with gradually red-shifted emission peaks in the two solvents with different polarities, which could be attributed to the intrinsic properties of CP-CDs such as heterogeneous surface compositions and states, as well as different sizes of CP-CDs [57]. All the above results further prove that the structure and fluorescence properties of CP-CDs are hardly changed when changing the solvent from methanol to water, except for the slight shift in the emission peak arised from solvent effect, corresponding to the results of TEM and DLS measurements.

The photostability of the as-prepared CP-CDs was further investigated to verify their practicability for possible sensing or bio-medical applications. Firstly, the influences of pH on the fluorescence property of CP-CDs was inspected under different pH conditions. As depicted in Fig. S4a, the CP-CDs fluorescence intensity at 436 nm with 360 nm excitation had no remarkable variation in the pH range from 3.0−9.0. Then, CP-CDs was continuously irradiated by xenon lamp and the corresponding fluorescence intensity almost remained constant during 120 min (Fig. S4b), proving the excellent photostability of them. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CP-CDs under different ionic strength was also determined. As exhibited in Fig. S4c, with the NaCl concentration increased constantly, no significant change could be observed. The results suggest that CP-CDs have outstanding photostability and are likely to be utilized as a high-performance fluorescence probe for optical or biological sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CP-CDs to Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the previous studies demonstrating that some metal ions can quench the fluorescence of numerous PL CDs effectively [4,58], we deduced that the as-prepared CP-CDs might also exhibit a sensitive fluorescence response to metal ions. To verify our speculation, the selectivity of CP-CDs for fluorescence detecting various metal ions was studied firstly. Seventeen metal ions (200 μM), including Na+, Mg2+, K+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cd2+, Ni+, Hg2+ and Pb2+, as well as five anions (200 μM) including CO3 2−, SO4 2−, IO4-, S2O3 2− and Cl- were mixed with 0.2 mg/mL CP-CDs solution in sodium acetate buffer (pH = 7.0), respectively. The fluorescence quenching effects of the metal ions and anions were shown in Fig. 4 . The results clearly demonstrated that only Cr(VI) exhibited strong fluorescence quenching effect on CP-CDs, while the fluorescence intensity of CP-CDs was hardly affected by other metal ions and anions. Moreover, the interference for the quenching effect was negligible when Cr(VI) coexist with other metal ions, indicating CP-CDs had outstanding selectivity fluorescence response toward Cr(VI).

In addition, as an important indicator to evaluate the practicability of CP-CDs in the field of sensing, the sensitivity of CP-CDs for detecting Cr(VI) was also studied. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the quenching of CP-CDs fluorescence gradually became obvious with increasing concentration of Cr(VI) and the CP-CDs fluorescence intensity was reduced by 95 % after the addition of Cr(VI) in 300 μM, demonstrating that CP-CDs had an extremely sensitive response to Cr(VI). The relationship between the fluorescent quenching ratio ((F0-F)/F0) and the Cr(VI) concentration ranged from 0.1–300 μM was illustrated in Fig. 5b. As shown in Fig. 5b inset, the value of (F0-F)/F0 exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9992) versus the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range of 0.03−50 μM. The detection limit of Cr(VI) was determined as 15 nM based on the formula of 3σ/slope, which was much lower than to the tolerable concentration of Cr(VI) in Cr(VI)-containing wastewater (9.6 μM). As listed in Table S3, compared with the sensing performance of the existing green CDs-based nanosensors and other fluorescent probes [59–65], the developed CP-CDs-based nanosensor has wider linear range and higher sensitivity for the detection of Cr(VI). Therefore, The results confirmed that CP-CDs possess huge application potential for detecting Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) to CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs can be classified into five main categories, including dynamic quenching, static quenching, Foster energy resonance transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET), according to the different molecular interactions between CDs and quenchers [66]. In order to obtain more insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) to CP-CDs, the fluorescence lifetime of CP-CDs and its possible variation were investigated. The CP-CDs fluorescence lifetime before and after the addition of Cr(VI) was shown in Fig. 6 a. Their average fluorescence lifetimes were 4.92 ns and 4.71 ns, respectively. The variation of fluorescence lifetime in the two dispersions was less than 0.5 ns and such slight difference was small enough to be disregarded. Thus, it was not possible for dynamic quenching or FRET. To further investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we also studied the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the fluorophore. As depicted in Fig. 6b, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) showed two characteristic absorption peaks at 255 nm and 354 nm. After the addition of the quencher Cr(VI), the UV–vis spectrum of CP-CDs/Cr(VI) mixed solution could basically match the summed UV–vis absorption spectrum of CP-CDs and Cr(VI), indicating that there was no new substance to form and the observed quenching effect of CP-CDs could not be ascribed to the static quenching process [67]. On the other hand, it is worth noting that the UV–vis absorption band of Cr(VI) was highly overlapped with the excitation wavelength of CP-CDs ranges from 280 nm to 410 nm (Fig. 3a). Therefore, we speculated that the IFE process should take into consideration. In order to further prove this speculation, the following Parker equation was used to calculate the corrected fluorescence intensity (Fcor) of CP-CDs:
(1)

where Fcor and Fobsd are the fluorescence intensities before and after taking IFE into consideration respectively. The absorbances at the maximum excitation and emission are represented by Aex (λ = 360 nm) and Aem (λ =436 nm), respectively. s is represented as the excitation beam thickness. g is denoted as the path length between the excitation beam edge and the cuvette edge. d represents the cuvette widths. After the values of Fcor were obtained, the fluorescence suppression efficiency (E) induced by the addition of different concentration of Cr(VI) could be determined by the following equation:
(2)

where Eobsd and Ecor are the fluorescence suppression efficiency after and before considering the IFE respectively. On this basis, the extent of IFE affecting the quenching effects could be evaluated by the correction factor (CF = Fcor/Fobsd) and the difference between Eobsd and Ecor. All the parameters needed for the calculation of Fcor and the value of CF under different Cr (VI) concentration have been tabulated in Table 1 . The CF value gradually increased as the concentration of Cr(VI) increased, indicating that the effect of IFE was continuously enhanced [68]. Moreover, the relationship between the values of Eobsd and Ecor versus different Cr(VI) concentration was illustrated in Fig. 6c. The suppressed efficiency caused by IFE reached about 90 % of the observed total suppressed efficiency, which proved the fluorescence quenching of Cr(VI) to CP-CDs was mainly derived from IFE. Therefore, based on these above results, the potential fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) to CP-CDs can be ascribed to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of the fluorescence sensing system in real complex samples, the quantitative analysis of Cr(VI) was conducted by standard addition method in three types of environment water samples (river, tap and lake water). Different concentrations of Cr(VI) (5 μM, 10 μM and 20 μM) were spiked into the sample solutions, respectively. The Cr(VI) concentrations of unspiked samples and spiked samples were analyzed by using the CP-CDs-based sensing system and ICP-OES system simultaneously. As listed in Table 2 , no Cr(VI) was detected in the unspiked environmental water samples using either CP-CDs-based sensing system or ICP-OES. The spiked recoveries of CP-CDs-based sensing system were distributed in 94.6–107.8% and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was lower than 3.7 %, which were comparable to the detection performance of ICP-OES system. Therefore, the CP-CDs-based sensing platform has good accuracy and high practicability for the detection of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site semi-quantitative visual detection and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in industrial sewage
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent sensitivity and selectivity of CP-CDs for sensing Cr (VI), a more simple, rapid and low cost strategy for on-site semiquantitative detection of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater was further developed based on the hybrid hydrogels consist of CP-CDs and agarose. The freshly prepared CP-CDs-based hydrogels were soaked into ultrapure water and no leakage of CP-CDs was observed, which indicated the strong immobilization effect of CP-CDs in the hydrogel matrices. The blue fluorescence of CP-CDs-based hydrogels could be quenched in different degrees under portable UV lamp when soaked them in the Cr(VI) solution under different conditions. In order to acquire the optimal sensing performance of the visual detection system, the CP-CDs concentration was optimized firstly. As shown in Fig. S5, the CP-CDs hydrogels doped with different concentrations of CP-CDs (1, 2 and 4 mg/mL) were soaked in different concentration of Cr(VI), respectively. When the CP-CDs concentration was 2 mg/mL, the highest sensitivity in the visual detection of Cr(VI) was achieved. Thus, the hydrogel with 2 mg/mL CP-CDs was utilized for the subsequent semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr(VI). Moreover, the kinetics of fluorescence change was also optimized. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence of CP-CDs-based hydrogels decreased gradually after soaking them in the solution containing 100 μM Cr(VI) within 30 min and when the incubating time was further extended, the quenching effect was hardly changed. Therefore, 30 min was allowed to be the incubating time in subsequent experiments.

Besides, the sensitivity and selectivity of the hybrid hydrogels-based chemical sensing platform were further studied. As shown in Fig. 7b and Fig. S7, in the absence of Cr(VI), the CP-CDs-based hydrogels exhibited the brightest blue fluorescence. With the increase of Cr(VI) concentration from 5 to 500 μM, the fluorescence of the CP-CDs-based hydrogels was gradually diminished or even completely quenched. Notably, the variation of the fluorescence intensity induced by the solution containing 10 μM Cr(VI), which was very close to the highest allowable Cr(VI) concentration of 9.6 μM, could be directly discerned by the naked eyes under ultraviolet light emitted from a commercial portable UV lamp. By contrast, as shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of CP-CDs-based hydrogel after soaking it in the vanadium extraction waste stream was significantly weaker than that induced by the reference solution, indicating that the concentration of Cr(VI) in the wastewater was higher than the discharge standard. Therefore, the developed CP-CDs-based hydrogels had great potential for the on-site semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr(VI) in industrial sewage. Fig. S7 showed the quenching effects of CP-CDs-based hydrogels in different kinds of metal ions solution (each 50 μM). The fluorescence of CP-CDs-based hydrogels could be only completely quenched by Cr(VI) and slightly quenched by some other metal ions. The results proved that the CP-CDs-based hydrogels have outstanding selectivity for detecting Cr(VI). Moreover, the anti-interference performance of CP-CDs-based hydrogels was further evaluated by using the real complex sample. After soaking CP-CD hydrogels in the water of Yanyu Lake (Luzhou, China) for 30 min, no visible change could be observed, demonstrating that the possible interference of other metal ions in complex water sample on the semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr(VI) could be negligible.

The quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in the sample of Cr(VI)-containing industrial sewage from vanadium extraction based on the CP-CDs-based sensing system in aqueous solution was further performed. The unknown amount of Cr(VI) in the industrial sewage was measured according to the standard curve. The calculated concentration of Cr(VI) was 31.4 μM, which was in good agreement with the result determined by ICP-OES (31.8 μM). Therefore, the results confirmed that the prepared fluorescent CP-CDs is applicable and reliable in the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in industrial sewage.

In summary, a green, facile and low energy consumption strategy using the one-step solvent extraction treatment to prepare green CDs from processed TCM under room temperature have been developed, which contributed to addressing the defects of traditional synthetic routes of biomass-derived CDs such as high energy-consuming, expensive heating devices, complicated and time-consuming synthesis processes and so on. The obtained CP-CDs exhibited outstanding fluorescence property and high photostability without any passivation or functionalization in CDs surface. Besides, the CP-CDs exhibited outstanding sensitivity and selectivity for Cr(VI) detection with wide linear range and extremely low detection limit of 15 nM, which could be successfully utilized for the quantitative detection of Cr(VI) in the environment water samples and industrial sewage with high precision. Moreover, the method using CP-CDs-based hydrogels as a detection platform for on-site semiquantitative determination of Cr (VI) in industrial wastewater was established successfully, which greatly enhanced the practicability of CP-CDs for sensing and environmental monitoring. Our research not only provides a facile and energy-efficient methodology for the green synthesis of fluorescent CDs and broadens the potential applications of processed TCM resources, but also holds great application prospects in the fields of sensing, environmental monitoring, bioimaging and clinical diagnosis.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Qiu is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. Her major research interests include the green synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and the preparation of non-toxic hair dye.

Die Gao is an associate professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. She received her PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology in 2017 from Chongqing University. Her research is focused on fluorescent sensor and the synthesis and application of novel COFs materials.
Honggang Yin is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. His major research interests include the synthesis and application of fluorescent organic nanoparticles.

Kailian Zhang is a professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. She received her bachelor's degree in Pharmaceutical Chemistry in 1988 from China Pharmaceutical University and master’s degree in Chinese and Western Integrative Medicine in 2006 from Southwest Medical University. Her major research interests include pharmaceutical analysis and drug metabolism.

Jing Zeng is an associate professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. His research is focused on the synthesis and application of novel chromatographic packing and the analytical methodologies on high-performance liquid chromatography.

Lujun Wang is an associate professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. He received his PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology in 2016 from Chongqing University. His research is focused on the novel separation materials of chromatography and stimuli-responsive materials.

Lan Xia is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. Her major research interests include the preparation of polydopamine-derived non-toxic hair dye and synthesis of fluorescent organic nanoparticles.

Kai Zhou is an engineer in Analytical and Testing Center, Chongqing University, China. His major research interests include the development of luminescent functionalized nanomaterials and the application of biological imaging.

Zhining Xia is a professor in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, China. He received his PhD degree in analytical chemistry from University of Copenhagen in 1995. During 1995–1996, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Faculty of Science, the University of Copenhagen. His research is focused on the development of chromatography and spectrometry analysis technologies.

Qifeng Fu is an associate professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. He received his bachelor's degree in Applied Chemistry in 2012 and PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology in 2017 from Chongqing University. His research is focused on the synthesis and application of novel fluorescent nanomaterials and the analytical methodologies on capillary electrophoresis.
